name: Cabeza de Arco Trail
name_original: Ruta de Cabeza de Arco — circular desde Caleao
description: Hike the difficult 8.3-mile Cabeza de Arco Trail in Redes Natural Park, gaining 2454 feet with diverse scenery and a natural arch.
region_intro: Hiking around Caso offers a diverse landscape characterized by mountainous terrain, dense forests, and river valleys. The region features a network of trails that traverse varying elevations, providing opportunities for both moderate and challenging hikes. These routes often lead through natural areas with significant elevation changes, indicative of a rugged environment. Best hiking trails around Caso The most popular hiking route is Tabayón de Mongallu Route, a 6.6 miles (10.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 23 minutes to complete. This route features a notable waterfall and forested paths. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cabeza de Arco Trail, a difficult 8.3 miles (13.4 km) path. This trail involves significant elevation gain, offering panoramic views from higher points. Local hikers also love the Tabayón del Mongallu Waterfall – Tarna loop from Tarna, a 5.4 miles (8.8 km) trail leading through a scenic loop, often completed in about 2 hours 50 minutes. Hiking around Caso is defined by mountainous paths, forested areas, and riverine landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate walks to more strenuous ascents. The routes in Caso are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 160 reviews. More than 1500 hikers have used komoot to explore Caso's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Caso?
A: Caso offers a diverse network of over 20 hiking trails. These routes traverse mountainous terrain, dense forests, and river valleys, providing options for various skill levels.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly hikes in Caso?
A: While many trails in Caso involve significant elevation changes, there are moderate options suitable for those looking for less strenuous ascents. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31855732" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tabayón del Mongallu Waterfall – Tarna loop from Tarna&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 5.4-mile (8.8 km) trail that can be completed in under 3 hours.

Q: Can I find trails with waterfalls in the Caso area?
A: Yes, the region is known for its riverine landscapes and features notable waterfalls. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1047543402/tabayon-de-mongallu-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tabayón de Mongallu Route&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 6.6-mile (10.7 km) trail specifically highlighted for its waterfall and forested paths.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Caso?
A: Yes, Caso offers several loop trails. A scenic option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31855732" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tabayón del Mongallu Waterfall – Tarna loop from Tarna&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 5.4-mile (8.8 km) circular route.

Q: What kind of views can I expect on hikes in Caso?
A: Hikes in Caso often lead to breathtaking panoramic views, especially from higher elevations. Trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1047553592/cabeza-de-arco-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabeza de Arco Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 8.3-mile (13.4 km) path, are known for offering expansive vistas of the surrounding mountainous landscape.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Caso?
A: The routes in Caso are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 160 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscape, from dense forests to rugged mountainous paths, and the rewarding views.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or attractions near the hiking trails?
A: Yes, the Caso area is rich in natural landmarks. You can explore various mountain passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7031801" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tarna Pass&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5543123" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Señales&lt;/a&gt;. There are also several peaks to discover, including &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4151061" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de la Cruz&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5430647" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Polinosa&lt;/a&gt;, offering additional exploration opportunities.

Q: What is the best time of year to hike in Caso for autumn colors?
A: The region's dense forests and mountainous terrain suggest that autumn would be a beautiful time to visit, with vibrant foliage. While specific peak times vary, late September to October typically offers the best display of autumn colors in similar European mountain regions.

Q: Are there any less crowded hiking options in Caso?
A: While popular routes like Tabayón de Mongallu attract many, the extensive network of over 20 trails means you can often find quieter paths. Exploring some of the more difficult or longer routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38175488" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Texu la Oración Viewpoint – Crestón Tunnel loop from Bezanes&lt;/a&gt;, might offer a more secluded experience due to their challenging nature.

Q: Are there any pubs or cafes near the hiking trails in Caso?
A: The region is characterized by small villages and natural areas. While specific establishments aren't listed in the trail data, it's common in such areas to find local eateries or pubs in the villages that serve as trailheads or are located along main access roads. It's advisable to check local listings for places like Tarna or Bezanes.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of hiking trails in Caso?
A: Caso offers a mix of difficulties. While there are 6 moderate trails, the majority (14 out of 20) are classified as difficult. This indicates a rugged environment with significant elevation changes, suitable for experienced hikers looking for a challenge.

Q: Is public transport available to reach hiking trails around Caso?
A: Information on specific public transport routes to trailheads is not detailed in the guide data. However, in many mountainous regions, local bus services connect villages. It is recommended to check local transport schedules for towns like Tarna or Bezanes, which are starting points for some trails.
tips: The Arrudos gorge is one of the great river canyons of Asturias and a very impressive and colorful route.

Caleao is a small village of houses perched on the mountainside, steep and cobbled paths, and intense green. Above the town, the landscape is made up of high mountain valleys, beautiful beech forests and animals such as wolves, deer, wild boars and wild cats. Higher up, huge stony and snow-capped peaks rise.

faqs.question: How long does it take to hike the Cabeza de Arco Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail typically takes around 5.5 to 6.5 hours to complete, though komoot estimates it can be done in about 5 hours and 20 minutes. This can vary based on your pace and how much time you spend enjoying the views or exploring the optional summit.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to visit is generally from late spring to early autumn. During winter, especially above 1,000 meters, you can expect snow, sometimes up to 40 cm deep, leading to slushy and muddy conditions during thawing. Overgrown sections can also be more challenging in late summer.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Cabeza de Arco Trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied. It starts with a mix of asphalt and well-defined paths, but as you ascend, it becomes more challenging with steep slopes. The descent, particularly from Collado del Arco, can be tricky and heavily overgrown with ferns and broom. You might also encounter muddy sections, especially in lower valleys or during snowmelt.

faqs.question: Are there any notable viewpoints or landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: Absolutely! The most iconic feature is the natural arch formation, Cabeza l'Arcu, which gives the trail its name. While the main PR-AS 124 route passes close by, many hikers opt for the additional steep ascent to the summit for panoramic views. Another spectacular highlight is the Foz de los Arrudos gorge. You'll also pass traditional cabins in areas like La Porquera and Majada el Felgueru.

faqs.question: Is the Cabeza de Arco Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Redes Natural Park generally welcomes dogs, it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially given the presence of wildlife and livestock. The varied and sometimes challenging terrain, including steep and overgrown sections, means your dog should be fit and accustomed to hiking.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Redes Natural Park?
faqs.answer: No, you do not typically need a permit or pay an entrance fee to hike the Cabeza de Arco Trail within the Redes Natural Park. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning wildlife and environmental protection.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail is a circular route that starts in Caleao Village. Parking is usually available in or near the village, which serves as the primary trailhead for this route.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route primarily follows the 'Ruta de Cabeza de Arco' (PR-AS 124). It also passes through significant sections of the 'Desfiladero de los Arrudos' and briefly touches upon the 'Sendero Histórico: Asturias'.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the hike?
faqs.answer: Komoot classifies this as a 'difficult' hike. The trail is approximately 13-17.5 kilometers long with significant elevation gain (around 750-970 meters). It features steep ascents, challenging descents, and potentially overgrown or muddy sections, making it suitable for experienced hikers.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenges or tips for hiking this route?
faqs.answer: Be prepared for varying conditions. The descent from Collado del Arco towards Majada el Felgueru can be particularly challenging due to overgrown paths. If you plan to tackle the optional climb to the summit of Cabeza l'Arcu, be aware it's a steep addition. Always carry sufficient water, especially as there are no guaranteed water sources on the trail itself, and wear appropriate footwear for muddy and uneven terrain.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I see on the trail?
faqs.answer: The Redes Natural Park is rich in biodiversity. While specific sightings are never guaranteed, you might encounter various bird species, deer, and other local fauna. Keep an eye out for signs of wildlife, especially in quieter sections of the trail.
